title: AWS CloudOps Private EC2 Operations Platform
shortDescription: Production-pattern EC2 fleet management with no public SSH, no bastion hosts, and no key pairs. Operator access exclusively through SSM Session Manager over VPC Interface Endpoints.
longDescription: Reference AWS CloudOps platform for operating EC2 workloads in private subnets the way well-run shops actually do it in 2026: SSM-only access, Golden AMIs via EC2 Image Builder, self-healing via ASG min=max=1, customer-managed KMS encryption, and AWS Backup with cold-storage lifecycle. Terraform modules, 7 ADRs documenting non-obvious decisions, and operational runbooks. Targets under $100/month in us-east-1.

architecture.summary: EC2 in private subnets, no internet gateway route for workloads. 5 VPC Interface Endpoints replace NAT for AWS service traffic. SSM is the only access path — no SSH keys provisioned. ASG min=max=1 provides self-healing at zero code cost. Image Builder + SSM Parameter Store + Launch Template decouple AMI ID from Terraform state.

operationalValue.problemSolved: Eliminates SSH key sprawl, bastion host maintenance, and stale AMI drift — the three most common EC2 security failures.
outcomes: Zero public-facing SSH endpoints — SSM Session Manager is the only access path
Full session audit trail to CloudWatch Logs via KMS-encrypted SSM logging
AMI pipeline automated — new AMIs published to SSM Parameter, picked up on next instance launch
Self-healing: ASG replaces failed instances automatically across AZs

metrics.costImpact: ~$55/month in us-east-1 — detailed breakdown in repo
metrics.reliabilityImpact: No bastion = no bastion outages or key rotation overhead

title: EKS Observability Stack — Prometheus & Grafana
shortDescription: End-to-end deployment runbook for a production EKS cluster with kube-prometheus-stack, IRSA-scoped IAM, HA Alertmanager, and ALB Ingress with ACM TLS.
longDescription: Production-grade, hardened deployment runbook for Amazon EKS with a fully integrated Prometheus + Grafana observability stack. Corrects the security model most tutorials get wrong: IRSA instead of node IAM roles, HA Alertmanager (2 replicas), PVC-backed storage for Prometheus and Grafana, TLS via ALB + ACM. Includes a deviation log documenting exactly what the reference source got wrong and why.

architecture.summary: EKS managed node groups in private subnets. kube-prometheus-stack via Helm — Prometheus Operator, Grafana (10Gi EBS), Prometheus (50Gi EBS, 15d retention), Alertmanager at 2 replicas. AWS Load Balancer Controller with IRSA service account. Grafana exposed via ALB Ingress with HTTPS-only and ACM certificate. Route 53 ALIAS to ALB.

operationalValue.problemSolved: Most EKS + Prometheus tutorials use node IAM roles (wrong blast radius), single Alertmanager (silent failure point), and no persistent storage. This runbook documents the correct production approach.
outcomes: IRSA scopes ALB Controller permissions to pod level — not the entire node
HA Alertmanager prevents silent alert loss during pod restarts
PVC-backed storage survives pod replacement and node rotation

metrics.reliabilityImpact: Zero dropped alerts during normal Alertmanager pod lifecycle

title: Container Runtime Runbooks — Homelabs
shortDescription: Production-minded runbooks for installing and configuring container runtimes on real hardware — CRI-O, crictl, LXD, and Podman across amd64 and arm64.
longDescription: Step-by-step runbooks for deploying container runtimes in homelab environments, executed and validated on physical hardware. Documents the gaps in upstream documentation for specific OS/architecture combinations — CRI-O on Linux Mint 20.2, Podman on arm64 Ubuntu. Every procedure includes root cause explanations for deviations from official docs. CKA preparation at the runtime level.

architecture.summary: Two physical machines with different architectures (amd64 and arm64) and different Linux distributions. Architecture diversity is intentional — surfaces package and driver issues that homogeneous environments hide.

operationalValue.problemSolved: CKA requires runtime-level knowledge that EKS abstracts away. This lab provides hands-on crictl and CRI-O experience at the level the exam and production incidents demand.
outcomes: CRI-O installed and validated on 4 OS/architecture combinations
crictl workflow documented for CKA runtime troubleshooting scenarios
Upstream documentation gaps corrected with root cause explanations
